author: @elye3701
text: You started off badly by not shielding the resistor{plastic bag?} before bashing it with a hammer and then you mistook a magnet for a ferrite rod. Good job you are not dealing with chemicals then. I make mine  with guitar wire wrapped around a strip of floor tile. No insulation because I want the best heat dissipation possible. So, some sort of cage around that.  For lower dissipation I suppose a test tube/jam bottle and fine dry sand would do the trick. You seriously need to improve your workshop skills.

author: @GeoffTV2
text: Question: Do wire wound resistors have inductance?

replies.author: @nicholasweiss4662
replies.text: Well, while every conductor has an inductance, it is true, that wire wound resistors have a specifically high inductances, as they are essentially a coil. Had he used a Ferrite core the Inductance would have been even Higher. These resistors are really only good for DC, or low Frequency AC and I'm assuming that he was actually going to use it as a DC shunt or dummy load.

replies.text: @nicholasweiss4662 Thank you for the reply, that was most helpful.

author: @sofa-lofa4241
text:
Luke, goggles are a must when teaching people stuff like this, ceramic shards and eyeballs don't get along very well, 
Please consider what you are doing and set a good example to people who don't know better,

I would also suggest fully enclosed 'machinist' goggles or full size face shield , the safety goggles that look like normal spectacles aren't much good,
I was a machinist (miller/turner) and always used fully enclosed goggles after a nasty experience when I had a tiny piece of swarf hit my cheek, bounced off the inside of my 'spectacle' style safety glasses and straight into my eye,
Luckily, I was 6 miles away from Maidstone eye hospital and as I was machining steel it  ould be pulled out with an electromagnet if it was aluminium/brass or any other non-ferrous material then I would have required eye surgery, not a nice thought!

I appreciate the stuff you're doing but please, please, set a good example

author: @jjmcrosbie
text:
What fun!
1 - You started off with the requirement for a 35R, 10W resistor.  That's 0.53A.  The relatively short length which you had,  (maybe 70cm?) must be a wire too fine for that current.  The wire inside the moulding would probably be OK if you got all the air out.  Have you run your resistor any length of time at 10W?  If necessary you might have to choose a greater length of thicker wire.  
2 - Also related to wire size, I was agreeably surprised to see you making a success of a crimp terminal onto such a fine wire.  One method of crimping a terminal onto fine wire is to use several thicknesses of the wire, ie allow extra length and wrap a few turns round a finger or pencil, slip it off and twiddle it into a stranded wire, them crimp that.
3 - Re getting all the air out, you might paint a somewhat runnier mix onto and into the winding, getting it right inside.   Leave it to dry and then pot it into your plastic mould.
4 - Re spacing turns of the wire to avoid shorted turns:  One way is to wrap two wires at a time, side by side, then remove the spacer wire.  The spacer wire need not be the expensive resistance wire.

5 - I suppose you might next have a go at setting it in an aluminium tube, taking steps to avoid contact from wire to tube.
And being picky, the Mi of "Mica" is pronounced 'My' and not 'Me'.  A good choice of material anyway.
6 -Anyway, well done in the first place.  It's something I never thought of doing.
Regards, Clive.

author: @stephengray9207
text: Hi Luke, Im sorry to say this but anyone can make a resistor of that resistance using resistance wire... what you didn't do was prove what current load it can sustain comfortably.  The example you compared it to handles 10 watts which means it can If it only copes with a tiny current it way too big. Please do some power handling tests in another video.  If you set the power supply to 18.7 volts into your resistor and it should have about 0.53 Amps and that would be 10 watts - dont start at that because it will likely fail very quickly. All you need to do is start at a low voltage, slowly crank the volts while measuring the temperature of the case you made. To be rated at 10 watts it must be capable of sustained ability to handle that load without failure. The issue with ceramic is that it moves the heat away from the wire. Because yours uses plaster of paris it probably won't spread the heat well. Thus yours will burn up the wire well before you get it to comfortably take 18 volts. Give it a go and see.

author: @station240
text: A ferrite core would have been a bad idea for a resistor anyway. Using something with magnetic properties results in a resistor more like an inductor, which is usually unwanted.

replies.text: Right on, unless making the more transformer I guess variac design that really isn't a variable resistor I learned again reading into a few days back.  Interesting are the "scariac" designs, albeit very much more risky.

author: @richardteychenne3950
text: Well done Luke, your communication and presentation skills are really coming on. Here is a question for you, in many applications low impedance power dissipation resistors are used and the heat they give off can be problematic to nearby components. Battery management systems for example. Could you as an alternative use the conductive ink on a painted (insulated) metal sheet to make a power resistor of just a few ohms which could be attached to the case side to dissipate heat away safely and quickly?

author: @scantrain5007
text:
Hi Luke,





it’s a
very nice project  and interesting too, because
those resistors are very useful  for
battery tests.





Just a
hint / suggestion:





If you
take a round base item like round wooden stick, glass or plastic. It’s depend
how hot comes the resistor. You can put the stick in a drill machine with
variable speed and lead the wire winding by winding on slow speed from the
machine.


 When this job done just cover the windings with paint for iron to fix them in place.
(No primer, they might be on led base and cause short cut or wrong resistance) 



 Where this suggestion  comes from? A long long time ago I build a radio detector.  The curl


was winded on the hard paper (carton) center tube from toilet paper. –It’s  not a joke!

text: One thing that you did not mention is what the power rating of the resistor actually is. i.e. 1W, 5W etc

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
Judging by its size and materials, I'd "guess", safely, maybe 5 watts.  

One could have tried to determin an appropriate power raiting given the materials and surface area and thermal conductivty of the insulators but it wont be of much use to everyone else who will likely vary in how they build their's and the kind of air flow they may have.  But these are good things to consider for your own power resistor in your application.
